subject: ap-chemistry
title: Acid-Base Titration & Buffer Chemistry
stimulus:
Potassium sorbate, KC H O (molar mass 150. g/mol) is commonly added to diet soft drinks as a preservative.
6 7 2
A stock solution of KC
$H_2O$(aq) of known concentration must be prepared. A student titrates 45.00 mLof the
stock solution with 1.25 M HCl(aq) using both an indicator and a pH meter. The value of $K_{a}$
for sorbic acid,
HC $H_2O$ , is 1.7 × 10$^{-5}$.
6 7 2

parts.prompt:
Write the net-ionic equation for the reaction between KC
$H_2O$(aq) and HCl(aq).

scoring_points.description: correct net-ionic equation for KC7H5O2(aq) + HCl(aq)

alternatives.correct_example: HC7H5O2−(aq) + H+(aq) → H2C7H5O2(aq) [or: HC7H5O2−(aq) + H3O+(aq) → H2C7H5O2(aq) + H2O(l)]

parts.prompt:
A total of 29.95 mL of 1.25 M HCl(aq) is required to reach the equivalence point. Calculate [KC
$H_2O$
] in
the stock solution.

scoring_points.description: correct calculated concentration of KHC7H5O2 in the stock solution

required_elements: Correct mathematical setup or formula application
Correct numerical answer with appropriate sig figs and units
alternatives.correct_example: At the equivalence point: moles HCl = moles KHC7H5O2. n(HCl) = (1.25 M)(0.02995 L) = 0.03744 mol. [KHC7H5O2] = 0.03744 mol / 0.04500 L = 0.832 M

parts.prompt:
The pHat the equivalence point of the titration is measured to be 2.54 Which of the following indicators
would be the best choice for determining the end point of the titration? Justify your answer.
pK
Indicator a
Phenolphthalein 9.3
Bromothymol blue 7.0
Methyl red 5.0
Thymol blue 2.0
Methyl violet 0.80

scoring_points.description: correct indicator selection based on pH at equivalence point of 2.54

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles
alternatives.correct_example: An indicator with a pKa near 2.54 should be selected, so that its color change occurs near the equivalence point pH. Methyl orange (pKa ≈ 3.5) or methyl violet would be appropriate.

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle

parts.prompt: Calculate the pH at the half-equivalence point.

scoring_points.description: correct pH at the half-equivalence point

required_elements: Correct mathematical setup or formula application
Correct numerical answer with appropriate sig figs and units
alternatives.correct_example: At the half-equivalence point, [A−] = [HA], so pH = pKa. The pKa of HC7H5O2− = −log(Ka). Using the given data, pH at half-equivalence point = pKa of potassium hydrogen phthalate ≈ 3.37.

parts.prompt:
The initial pH and the equivalence point are plotted on the graph below. Accurately sketch the titration curve
on the graph below. Mark the position of the half-equivalence point on the curve with an X.

The pH of the soft drink is 3.37 after the addition of the KC
$H_2O$(aq). Which species, HC
$H_2O$
or
C $H_2O$ − , has a higher concentration in the soft drink? Justify your answer.
6 7 2

scoring_points.description: correct identification of the dominant species at pH 3.37

required_elements: Valid justification based on chemical principles
alternatives.correct_example: At pH 3.37 = pKa, [HC7H5O2−] = [H2C7H5O2], meaning equal concentrations of both species. The pH = pKa indicates the half-equivalence point where acid and conjugate base are present in equal amounts.

common_traps: Stating answer without connecting to the underlying chemical principle
